questions.question_text: A Maine licensee takes a verbal listing from a homeowner. Is this listing enforceable?
questions.correct_answer: No, listings must be in writing
distractors: Only if witnessed by a third party
questions.explanation: Maine requires all listing and brokerage contracts to be in writing to be enforceable. A verbal listing agreement, regardless of the parties' intent, cannot be enforced in court. This protects both the seller and the brokerage from disputes over terms.

questions.question_text: Who must authorize and sign a listing agreement in a Maine brokerage?
questions.explanation: In Maine, the designated broker (DB) must authorize and sign all listing agreements. The DB holds ultimate responsibility for all brokerage contracts and operations. While an associate may prepare the agreement, the DB's authorization is required.

questions.question_text: A seller signs an exclusive right-to-sell listing. She then finds her own buyer without agent help. Does she owe a commission?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the listing broker earns it
distractors: Only if the broker showed the home
questions.explanation: Under an exclusive right-to-sell listing, the broker earns the commission regardless of who procures the buyer, even if the seller finds the buyer independently. This is the most common and most protective listing type for brokers in Maine.

questions.question_text: What must a Maine listing agreement identify?
distractors: Only the broker and commission rate
Only the seller and listing period
questions.explanation: A valid Maine listing agreement must identify the parties involved, the property being listed, the terms of the agreement (including duration), and the compensation arrangement. Missing any essential element could render the agreement unenforceable.

questions.question_text: Under which listing type can a Maine seller hire multiple brokers and only pay the one who produces the buyer?
questions.explanation: An open listing allows the seller to engage multiple brokers simultaneously, with only the broker who actually procures the buyer earning a commission. The seller can also find their own buyer and owe no commission. This offers the least protection for brokers.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller agree on a home price over the phone but never sign anything. Under Maine's statute of frauds, is this contract enforceable?
questions.correct_answer: No, real estate contracts must be written
distractors: Yes, if a witness confirms the call
questions.explanation: Maine's statute of frauds requires all contracts for the sale of real estate to be in writing and signed by the parties to be enforceable. A verbal agreement, no matter how specific, cannot be enforced in a Maine court for a real property transaction.

questions.question_text: Which standard form is commonly used for residential purchase agreements in Maine?
questions.explanation: The Maine Association of REALTORS (MAR) Purchase and Sale Agreement is the standard form used for residential transactions in Maine. It includes provisions for identifying parties, property, price, closing date, and common contingencies.

questions.question_text: A buyer's offer includes a financing contingency. The lender denies the loan. What happens to the contract?
questions.correct_answer: Buyer may cancel and recover the deposit
distractors: Buyer must immediately find a different lender
Seller automatically keeps the earnest money deposit
Contract converts to an all-cash purchase sale
questions.explanation: A financing contingency protects the buyer if they cannot obtain the specified loan. When the contingency is properly invoked and the buyer is denied financing, the buyer may cancel the contract and receive a return of their earnest money deposit. Common contingencies in Maine include financing, inspection, title, and disclosure review.

questions.question_text: A Maine purchase agreement must include which essential elements?
distractors: Only buyer name and loan amount
Only closing date and title company
questions.explanation: A valid Maine purchase and sale agreement must identify the parties (buyer and seller), the property, the purchase price, and the closing date. Additional common provisions include contingencies for inspection, financing, title examination, and disclosure review.

questions.question_text: A buyer requests an attorney review period in a Maine sales contract. What is the purpose of this provision?
questions.correct_answer: Allow legal counsel to review terms
distractors: Delay closing for a full 60 days
Transfer all liability to the attorney
questions.explanation: An attorney review period gives the buyer (or seller) time to have legal counsel review the contract terms before they become fully binding. This is a common provision in Maine purchase agreements and allows either party to propose modifications or withdraw based on legal advice.

questions.question_text: Two competing brokerages in Portland charge the same commission rate. Is this a violation?
questions.correct_answer: Only if they agreed to fix rates
distractors: No, identical rates are always perfectly legal
Yes, commission rates must always differ
Only if formally reported to the Commission
questions.explanation: Real estate commissions in Maine are fully negotiable. Two brokerages independently charging the same rate is not itself a violation. However, if brokerages conspire or agree to fix commission rates, that constitutes illegal price-fixing under antitrust law.

questions.question_text: When is a broker's commission typically considered earned in Maine?
questions.correct_answer: When a ready, willing, able buyer is produced
distractors: Only after the transaction closing is officially complete
When the listing agreement is first signed by seller
When the buyer obtains full mortgage financing approval
questions.explanation: In Maine, a broker's commission is generally considered earned when the broker produces a buyer who is ready, willing, and able to purchase on the seller's terms. The commission may be payable even if the transaction does not close, depending on the listing agreement terms.

questions.question_text: After the August 2024 NAR Settlement, how are buyer agent commissions handled on Maine MLS listings?
questions.correct_answer: No longer offered through the MLS
distractors: Still displayed as a required field
Paid directly by the state fund
questions.explanation: Following the NAR Settlement effective August 2024, buyer agent compensation is no longer displayed or offered through MLS listings. Buyer agents must negotiate their compensation separately, either through buyer-broker agreements or other arrangements outside the MLS system.

questions.question_text: A Maine licensee prepares a CMA for a seller. How must the CMA be presented?
questions.explanation: In Maine, a comparative market analysis must not be represented as an appraisal. The licensee must disclose that they are not a licensed appraiser. A CMA is an informal estimate of market value based on comparable sales, distinct from a formal appraisal performed by a licensed appraiser.

questions.question_text: A seller insists that the agent guarantee a specific commission rate applies to all brokerages. How should the agent respond?
distractors: Agree to set a standard rate
Refer to the state fee schedule
questions.explanation: Maine law requires that commissions be negotiable. No agent can guarantee that all brokerages charge the same rate, and suggesting otherwise could constitute an antitrust violation. The agent should explain that each brokerage independently sets its own compensation terms.
